(right) Multi-Head Attention consists of several attention layers running in parallel. query with all keys, divide each by √dk, and apply a softmax function to obtain the weights on the values.
script.narration: In this section, we introduce the concept of Scaled Dot-Product Attention. As shown in the diagram, this mechanism involves querying with all keys, dividing each by the square root of dk, and applying a softmax function to obtain the weights on the values. This process is fundamental to understanding how attention mechanisms work in transformers.

script.narration: In practice, we compute the attention function on multiple queries at once. These queries are packed together into a matrix Q. Similarly, the keys and values are packed into matrices K and V. This allows us to compute the matrix of outputs efficiently.

script.section_text: The two most commonly used attention functions are additive attention [2], and dot-product (multiplicative) attention. Dot-product attention is identical to our algorithm, except for the scaling factor of 1/√dk. Additive attention computes the compatibility function using a feed-forward network with a single hidden layer. While the two are similar in theoretical complexity, dot-product attention is much faster and more space-efficient in practice, since it can be implemented using highly optimized matrix multiplication code.
script.narration: Let's compare two commonly used attention functions: additive attention and dot-product attention. Dot-product attention is similar to our algorithm, but includes a scaling factor of 1 over the square root of dk. Additive attention, on the other hand, uses a feed-forward network with a single hidden layer to compute the compatibility function. Although both methods are similar in theoretical complexity, dot-product attention is generally faster and more space-efficient in practice. This is because it can leverage highly optimized matrix multiplication code.

In practice, we compute the attention function on a set of queries simultaneously, packed together into a matrixQ. The keys and values are also packed together into matricesK andV . We compute the matrix of outputs as:
Attention(Q,K,V ) = softmax(QK T

The two most commonly used attention functions are additive attention [2], and dot-product (multiplicative) attention. Dot-product attention is identical to our algorithm, except for the scaling factor of 1√dk. Additive attention computes the compatibility function using a feed-forward network with a single hidden layer. While the two are similar in theoretical complexity, dot-product attention is much faster and more space-efficient in practice, since it can be implemented using highly optimized matrix multiplication code.
While for small values ofdk the two mechanisms perform similarly, additive attention outperforms dot product attention without scaling for larger values ofdk [3]. We suspect that for large values of dk, the dot products grow large in magnitude, pushing the softmax function into regions where it has extremely small gradients 4. To counteract this effect, we scale the dot products by 1√dk.
3.2.2 Multi-Head Attention
Instead of performing a single attention function with dmodel-dimensional keys, values and queries, we found it beneficial to linearly project the queries, keys and values h times with different, learned linear projections to dk, dk and dv dimensions, respectively. On each of these projected versions of queries, keys and values we then perform the attention function in parallel, yielding dv-dimensional output values. These are concatenated and once again projected, resulting in the final values, as depicted in Figure 2.
Multi-head attention allows the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions. With a single attention head, averaging inhibits this.
script.narration: In this section, we discuss scaling dot products for large values of dk. The figure shows two diagrams: Scaled Dot-Product Attention on the left and Multi-Head Attention on the right. Multi-Head Attention consists of several attention layers running in parallel. To compute the attention, we query with all keys, divide each by the square root of dk, and apply a softmax function to obtain the weights on the values. In practice, we compute the attention function on a set of queries simultaneously, packed into matrices Q, K, and V. The output matrix is computed as Attention(Q, K, V) equals softmax of QK transpose over the square root of dk, multiplied by V. Dot-product attention, which is faster and more space-efficient, is used with a scaling factor to prevent large dot products from pushing the softmax function into regions with extremely small gradients. Multi-head attention projects queries, keys, and values multiple times and performs the attention function in parallel, allowing the model to attend to information from different subspaces at different positions.

script.section_text: 3.2.2 Multi-Head Attention Instead of performing a single attention function with dmodel-dimensional keys, values and queries, we found it beneficial to linearly project the queries, keys and values h times with different, learned linear projections to dk, dk and dv dimensions, respectively. On each of these projected versions of queries, keys and values we then perform the attention function in parallel, yielding dv-dimensional output values. These are concatenated and once again projected, resulting in the final values, as depicted in Figure 2. Multi-head attention allows the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions. With a single attention head, averaging inhibits this.
script.narration: Instead of using a single attention function with high-dimensional keys, values, and queries, multi-head attention projects these elements multiple times using different learned linear projections. This process results in lower-dimensional versions of queries, keys, and values. The attention function is then performed in parallel on each of these projected versions, producing lower-dimensional output values. These outputs are concatenated and projected once more to obtain the final values, as shown in the diagram. Multi-head attention enables the model to simultaneously focus on information from various representation subspaces at different positions, an advantage not possible with a single attention head.

script.narration: Multi-head attention offers a significant advantage. It enables the model to simultaneously focus on information from various representation subspaces at different positions. This is not possible with a single attention head, where averaging can limit this capability.

script.section_text: To illustrate why the dot products get large, assume that the components of q and k are independent random variables with mean 0 and variance 1. Then their dot product, q · k = ∑dk i=1 qiki, has mean 0 and variance dk.
script.narration: To understand why dot products can get large, let's assume that the components of vectors q and k are independent random variables, each with a mean of 0 and a variance of 1. Under these conditions, the dot product of q and k, which is the sum of the products of their corresponding components, will have a mean of 0 and a variance of d times k. This variance is what causes the dot product to potentially become very large.
